reviewer: build-wave agent (real headless-Chrome first-visit download → inference + cached-reload auto-init verification, revision 1)

rubric.evidence: Real first-visit run in headless Chrome (WASM, no-GPU): loader state 'download-required' → clicked 'Download model (~136 MB)' → reached state=ready → the page auto-scored the default prize-scam message → vLabel 'SPAM', confidence 99.9%, backend WASM. Standalone transformers.js@3.7.5 check (top_k:2) returned spam 0.9994 / not_spam 0.0006 on two scam messages and not_spam 0.9997 on two genuine messages. Console + network clean (0 errors, 0 failures). Screenshot: /home/paulkinlan/tmp/verify-new/spam-detection-desktop.png.
rubric.notes: Inference runs off the main thread in models/spam-detection/worker.js via loadPipeline (text-classification, onnx-community/tanaos-spam-detection-v1-ONNX, q8).

rubric.evidence: Output is a 2-class softmax (spam / not_spam) summing to ~1; verified semantically correct in both directions — scam/phishing text → SPAM ≥0.999, conversational/work text → HAM ≥0.9997. top_k:2 confirmed to return both classes (default returns only the winner); worker reconstructs spam from not_spam when only the winner is returned (binary softmax).
rubric.notes: spamProb() indexes by label so class ordering never matters; renderClassBars shows both real probabilities; the occlusion attribution is in SPAM log-odds.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance table (desktop screenshot) shows the exact model id onnx-community/tanaos-spam-detection-v1-ONNX linked to its card, task text-classification, backend WebAssembly, quantisation q8, ~136 MB, MIT. conformance: declares-model-id / declares-task / declares-backend / declares-quantisation / inference-off-main-thread all derive-pass on the real metadata.
rubric.notes: Worker uses new Worker + worker.js; no main-thread inference.

rubric.evidence: Cached-reload test (same profile, Cache Storage persists): the page AUTO-initialised to state=ready with NO Download click; the only action button shown was 'Clear cached model' (Download button present: false). Verified programmatically and by screenshot /home/paulkinlan/tmp/verify-new/spam-detection-cached.png.
rubric.notes: Uses the shared lib/model-loader.js createModelLoader auto-init architecture; no hand-rolled Load button.

rubric.evidence: live-status-region=pass (role=status aria-live on #runStatus/#attrStatus and the loader status), errors-on-the-page=pass (scoring/attribution/triage failures write to an .err status). Console clean on first visit AND cached reload (0 errors). runs-without-webgpu-fallback: this is a WASM-only model — it ran fully on the no-GPU runner, no WebGPU needed. Download progress surfaced via the shared loader <progress> (observed 'Downloading model … %').
rubric.notes: Retry path provided by createModelLoader on failure; triage board fills progressively as per-item results land.

rubric.evidence: Main page: textarea (labelled), 4 example chips (data-set), 'Explain the current message' occlusion button, plus the loader's Download/Clear buttons. Practical: inbox textarea, 'Triage inbox', 'Load sample inbox'. Wild: original textarea + 5 obfuscation checkboxes in a labelled fieldset. Multi-model: two loaders, gate + summarize buttons, length range. All real, labelled, keyboard-operable. buttons-have-names=pass.
rubric.notes: Occlusion attribution runs N+1 batched forward passes in the worker; batch triage streams per-item.

rubric.evidence: Every visible verdict comes from the on-device pipeline; verified by watching the default (SPAM 99.9%) and standalone runs produce genuinely different, semantically correct probabilities. No canned label strings in the source; sample messages are inputs, not outputs.

images-have-alt=pass (no content images), buttons-have-names=pass. Meter labels sit OUTSIDE the coloured bars (meter-labels / class-bars carry numeric values) to keep WCAG AA contrast; class names use the design-system good/bad tokens. Live regions announce status. Light + dark both legible: /home/paulkinlan/tmp/verify-new/spam-detection-{desktop,dark}.png.
rubric.notes: Attribution word chips carry a title with the exact log-odds; badge colours pair with text labels (not colour alone).

rubric.evidence: responsive-check (automated): desktop (1280×800) and mobile (360×740 DPR3) both clean — no horizontal overflow, no clipped controls, console+network clean across index + basics + practical + wild + multi-model. Screenshots: reports/responsive/screens/spam-detection/{desktop,mobile}.png. Triage board uses auto-fit minmax columns that collapse to one column on mobile; support recorded per the responsive-check result.
rubric.notes: Logical properties throughout; chips wrap; triage-text uses overflow-wrap:anywhere so long junk URLs never overflow.

rubric.evidence: All inference (single classify, batch triage, and N+1 occlusion) runs in a Web Worker; the main thread only renders small DOM updates. Typing is debounced (350ms). Batch triage posts each result as it lands so a large paste fills progressively instead of blocking. No blocking loop on the main thread; INP stays low.
rubric.notes: guidance break-up-long-tasks applied via the worker + progressive per-item posting.

guidanceConsulted.query: responsive control panel grid without horizontal overflow on mobile and desktop
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Grid repeat(auto-fit, minmax()) for card/column rows; flexbox with flex-wrap + min-inline-size:0 for long content; logical properties; avoid fixed widths that overflow narrow viewports.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — the triage board and comparison layouts use auto-fit minmax() grids that collapse to one column on mobile; class-bar rows use a fixed label column + flexible track; triage-text uses overflow-wrap:anywhere. Verified no overflow at 360px and 1280px.

guidanceConsulted.query: keep input responsive INP break up long tasks web worker
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move heavy work off the main thread and yield; keep the UI responsive during long processing.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — all spam inference (single, batch, and the N+1 occlusion pass) runs in a Web Worker; the batch posts each per-message result as it lands so the main thread only ever does small DOM appends; typing is debounced.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: conformance inference-off-main-thread=pass; console clean during live scoring + batch triage.

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible loading progress and error retry states live region
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Announce status/errors in an accessible live region next to the action; label every control; keep colour paired with text.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — role=status aria-live=polite on the run/attr/triage status lines and the loader status; failures add an .err class the user can read; spam/ham badges pair colour with a text label.

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible form textarea range slider controls labels
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Associate labels with controls; group related toggles in a fieldset/legend.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — textareas have wrapping <label>s; the wild-page obfuscation toggles sit in a <fieldset> with a <legend>; range sliders on the multi-model page have labelled value read-outs.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: wild/index.html fieldset+legend; multi-model length range with live val.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use cross-document view transitions for catalogue↔page navigation.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — @view-transition navigation:auto in public/styles.css (shared design system, invariant 6).

openQuestions: The wild page shows the RAW un-normalised model; should it also offer a 'normalise then classify' toggle (strip homoglyphs / collapse spacing) to demonstrate the real-world defence, or keep that as a separate demo?
Is a binary spam/ham split the right framing, or would a 'promotional vs scam vs genuine' three-way (needs a different model) be more useful to show?

followUpGoals.goal: Add a spam-detection 'normalise-then-classify' wild variant that strips homoglyphs / zero-width chars before scoring, showing the standard filter defence against the obfuscation attack.
Deeper mobile tap-target + focus-order audit on the triage board and obfuscation checkboxes at 360px to flip support from needs-review to ok with recorded evidence.

summary: Revision-1 critique for spam-detection (onnx-community/tanaos-spam-detection-v1-ONNX, text-classification, WASM q8, ~136 MB, MIT), backed by a real headless first-visit download → live output run (SPAM 99.9% on a prize-scam message), a cached-reload auto-init check (no Download button, 'Clear cached model' offered), a standalone transformers.js check confirming spam ≥0.999 / ham ≥0.9997 across four messages, conformance 14✓/0✗/9 manual, and a clean desktop+mobile responsive scan. The tanaos DistilBERT was chosen after the more-downloaded bert-tiny SMS model misclassified a phishing message as ham and the OTIS ONNX was found to be exported with a hard-coded 9-token input; tanaos was correct and confident on every test. Strong, honest demo; mobile tap-target depth left as a follow-up.
